evidence: Package layout (api, config, exploration, indexer, model, query, utils, viz) maps each folder to a named domain capability with no historical-accident names visible.
model/package-info.java explicitly documents the package boundary, the two allowed type categories (DTOs vs model objects with derived accessors), and prohibits I/O concerns.
GrammarConfig (data) and GrammarConfigLoader (I/O) are split into separate classes; the javadoc of each explains the separation intent.
API handler decomposition into SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers each owns one coherent HTTP concern.
Options-object pattern (ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions) applied consistently for extensible parameter passing.

RelationType enum (SURFACE, DEP, ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF) conflates two orthogonal concepts: dispatch strategy (SURFACE/DEP used in SketchHandlers for routing) and semantic role labels (ADJ_PREDICATE etc. used in CollocateProfileComparator and SemanticFieldExplorer for filtering). A new engineer reading the enum cannot tell which values are routing tags and which are semantic category filters.

RelationConfig javadoc states deriveDeprel() 'is only called during construction and has no callers outside this class' but SketchHandlers calls it in three places (lines 94, 148, 274), contradicting the documented ownership contract.

RelationConfig javadoc references GrammarConfig#getRelation but the actual method is named GrammarConfig#relation(String), a documentation-to-reality mismatch for a cross-reference specifically meant to explain API design rationale.

All five handler classes (SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) are uniformly package-private with no access modifier on the class declaration — consistent visibility discipline across the api package.

Every handler that processes a request uses the identical structural protocol: a package-private logger field, injected dependencies in a package-private constructor, package-private handle* methods, and a private inner record for request-parameter grouping (ConcordanceExamplesRequest, BcqlRequest, ExplorationParams, ExecutedSketch) — a strongly consistent sibling pattern.

parseSeedSet in ExplorationHandlers.java line 233 is annotated @NonNull String seedsParam but line 234 immediately checks `if (seedsParam == null || seedsParam.isEmpty())` — the @NonNull annotation promises callers the parameter is non-null, making the null branch dead code. Two contradictory null-handling signals coexist in the same method signature.

Only the model package has a package-info.java; the api, config, exploration, query, indexer, and utils packages have none. The convention of documenting package boundaries is applied to exactly one package out of seven, rather than consistently or not at all.
Naming is fully consistent throughout: PascalCase class names, camelCase method names, SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE constants — no snake_case drift, no style islands.

The model package deliberately mixes records (CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, AdjectiveProfile, Edge, ExplorationOptions, etc.) with plain classes (ComparisonResult, ExplorationResult) for documented reasons: both classes explain in their Javadoc and in model/package-info.java why they are classes rather than records. This is intentional documented variation, not drift.

HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling maps IllegalArgumentException → 400, IOException → 500, RequestEntityTooLargeException → 413, and all other Throwable → 500; applied uniformly to every route in WordSketchApiServer

All handler methods (ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) declare throws IOException and throw IllegalArgumentException for client errors — no handler returns null or swallows exceptions

BlackLabSnippetParser uses @Nullable-annotated return-null for its parsing utilities (extractCollocateLemma, extractCollocateFromXmlByPosition, etc.) — an honest contract for a text parser where 'not found' is normal
GrammarConfigLoader has a documented two-tier contract: load(Path) throws IOException, createDefaultEnglish() wraps to IllegalStateException for startup/DI callers — intentional and commented

executeRelationQueries in SketchHandlers intentionally catches per-relation IOException, logs a warning, and accumulates into a response.errors field; partial-failure is surfaced to the client rather than swallowed

Main.java CLI uses bare Double.parseDouble / Integer.parseInt on --min-logdice and --port without catching NumberFormatException; they fall through to the top-level catch (Exception e) which prints a generic 'Application error' message rather than a user-friendly IAE-style message
CollocateQueryHelper.executeCollocateSearchImpl wraps InvalidQuery (BlackLab runtime) into IOException to give callers a consistent checked exception — consistent boundary conversion

Class names are domain-precise throughout: SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator, MultiSeedExplorer, BlackLabSnippetParser, RelationPatternBuilder, CollocateQueryHelper — each communicates its role without ambiguity.
Handler method names follow a consistent handle<Domain><Concept> convention: handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldComparison, handleConcordanceExamples, handleFullDependencySketch.

In SemanticFieldExplorer.identifyCoreCollocates() and filterByMinShared(), local variables use the 'colloc' abbreviation (collocFrequency, collocTotalScore, collocScores) while the rest of the codebase consistently uses 'collocate' (collocateLemma, collocateFreq, collocateTotal, collocatePos).

ConcordanceHandlers.ConcordanceExamplesRequest record renames HTTP params seed->noun and collocate->adjective in its fields. 'seed' and 'collocate' are the system-wide vocabulary; 'adjective' is a more specific POS term that doesn't reflect the relation-agnostic nature of the endpoint.

SketchHandlers contains thin one-liner wrapper methods with distinct names: handleFullDependencySketch() solely calls handleDepSketch(); handleFullSketch() solely calls handleFullSketchForType() with SURFACE; handleRelationSketch() solely calls handleRelationQueryForPattern() with SURFACE. Each adds a name-change hop with no new information.

In SemanticFieldExplorer.buildCollocateToNounsMap, the return variable is named 'nounProfiles' (Map<String, Map<String, Double>> keyed by noun) while the method is named 'buildCollocateToNounsMap', implying a collocate-keyed direction. The two names encode opposite traversal directions for the same map.
Constants are self-documenting: DEFAULT_SKETCH_RESULTS, SINGLE_RELATION_RESULTS, OVER_FETCH_FACTOR, MAX_PARAM_LENGTH, DEFAULT_CORS_ALLOW_ORIGIN.

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed (line 357-362) is a single-line delegation: `return multiSeedExplorer.findCollocateIntersection(seeds, relationConfig, opts.minLogDice(), opts.topCollocates(), opts.minShared());` — its only work is unwrapping the ExplorationOptions record; no validation, no translation, no policy.

SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles (line 316-322) is similarly a one-line delegation after a guard: `return comparator.compareCollocateProfiles(seedNouns, opts.minLogDice(), opts.topCollocates());` — the ExplorationOptions wrapper is unpacked and discarded immediately.

SemanticFieldExplorer Javadoc explicitly labels the class 'a thin facade delegating to both this class and CollocateProfileComparator', confirming the pass-through design is intentional rather than incidental.

SketchHandlers.handleFullSketch (line 105-107) is a one-liner: `handleFullSketchForType(exchange, lemma, RelationType.SURFACE)` — a private naming alias for a hardcoded constant argument, adding a call depth with zero behavior.
SketchHandlers.handleFullDependencySketch (line 114-116) mirrors the same pattern: `handleDepSketch(exchange, lemma)` — a private rename wrapper.

QueryExecutor interface (query/QueryExecutor.java) has exactly one production implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor). The interface Javadoc self-identifies this as a 'testability seam rather than an abstraction boundary', making it an honest single-impl interface.

The remaining abstractions — ExploreResponseAssembler (model-to-JSON translation), GrammarConfigSerializer (keeps JSON library out of config records), CollocateQueryHelper (encapsulates collocate scoring/ranking), RelationPatternBuilder (keeps RelationConfig a pure data carrier), HttpApiUtils (enforces CORS, method checking, size limits) — all carry real behavioral weight that justifies their existence.

Handler decomposition (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) is motivated by domain separation, not arbitrary splitting; each class has a coherent concern.
No util/helper dumping grounds, delegation-heavy classes, wide options bags, or facade/re-export modules were found in the codebase.
pom.xml declares 5 runtime dependencies: blacklab-engine, blacklab-query-parser, fastjson2, slf4j-api, logback-classic, jspecify — all are directly used in production code
fastjson2 is used across 15 import sites in 5+ files (CorpusQueryHandlers, GrammarConfigSerializer, HttpApiUtils, SketchHandlers, VisualizationHandlers, GrammarConfigLoader) — heavy, justified use
jspecify is imported in 17 files for @Nullable/@NonNull annotations — lightweight annotation-only dep, used consistently
SLF4J 2.0.7 + logback-classic 1.5.18 — logback 1.5.x requires SLF4J 2.x, pairing is correct; no version conflict

BlackLab resolved from raw GitHub raw-content URL: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/instituutnederlandsetaal/BlackLab/mvn-repo/ — pom.xml comment acknowledges this has no SLA and can break CI at any time

blacklab-query-parser declared explicitly alongside blacklab-engine; it is directly used (ContextualQueryLanguageParser in BlackLabQueryExecutor, CorpusQueryLanguageParser in CollocateQueryHelper) — explicit declaration is justified regardless of whether it is already transitive
No duplicate JSON libraries (no Jackson, Gson, or org.json alongside fastjson2); no duplicate HTTP client libraries
HTTP server built on com.sun.net.httpserver (JDK built-in) — no external HTTP server dependency needed; appropriate for an embedded lightweight server

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern decomposes cleanly into four named phase methods: fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates — each focused and appropriately sized
CollocateQueryHelper uses a two-phase BCQL pipeline (collectHits → scoreHits) with explicit state boundaries and pre-fetch of unique collocate frequencies to avoid redundant calls
BlackLabSnippetParser.extractHeadword and findLabelTokenIndex operate on raw BCQL pattern strings (not XML snippets), mixing two distinct concerns inside a class named for XML snippet parsing

ConlluConverter uses a long[] state array with int-constant indices (STATE_SENTENCES, STATE_TOKENS, STATE_CHUNKS) as a mutable accumulator passed across method boundaries — readable with constants but more fragile than an encapsulated mutable state object

rotateChunkIfNeeded signals whether rotation occurred by returning null vs. new long[]{newChunkCount}, forcing the caller to null-check and manually reset writer and sentencesInChunk — intent is obscured
ExplorationHandlers.parseSeedSet is annotated @NonNull yet opens with `if (seedsParam == null || seedsParam.isEmpty())` — a defensive null guard that contradicts the contract annotation

SketchHandlers.executeRelationQueries uses a BiFunction<RelationConfig, ExecutedSketch, Map<String,Object>> callback but has only two call sites with non-trivial lambda bodies, making control flow harder to trace than two focused loops would be
Guard clauses are used consistently across all API handler methods (null/empty lemma checks before query dispatch, parameter length guards before processing)

Helper extraction is consistent: CollocateQueryHelper owns collocate ranking, RelationPatternBuilder owns pattern construction, ExploreResponseAssembler owns model-to-JSON translation — no method does two jobs

ExploreResponseAssembler.buildExplorationEdges cross-products result.seeds() × result.seedCollocates() to produce SEED_ADJ edges (lines 40-45). In multi-seed mode, result.seedCollocates() is a merged map built by MultiSeedExplorer taking Math.max across all seeds, so a collocate found only for 'theory' also receives an edge from 'model'. The Javadoc comment claims edges 'correctly name their source' but the per-seed origin is lost at the merge step.

ComparisonResult.summaryCounts() re-implements the fully-shared/partially-shared/specific classification inline (p == total, p >= 2, else) instead of delegating to AdjectiveProfile.sharingCategory(). The same branching logic already lives in AdjectiveProfile.isFullyShared/isPartiallyShared/isSpecific. Two independent definitions of the same threshold.

ExplorationHandlers.populateComparisonResponse is called with (topCollocates, minLogDice, minShared) as three individual primitive arguments decomposed from exploreParams. The ExplorationOptions record is constructed earlier in the same method and carries exactly these three fields but is not passed to the assembler. populateExploreResponse, by contrast, operates on an ExplorationResult rather than receiving these primitives, making the two assembler entry-points structurally inconsistent.

ExplorationHandlers.resolveRelationConfig validates that relationType is non-empty and throws IllegalArgumentException if absent, but callers still call relationConfig.relationType().orElseThrow() immediately after the return (lines ~73 and ~106 in ExplorationHandlers). The comment 'Safe: resolveRelationConfig guarantees relationType is present' patches over the structural duplication of the unwrap.

SketchHandlers lines 105-115: three private single-line pass-through methods — handleFullSketch calls handleFullSketchForType(exchange, lemma, RelationType.SURFACE); handleRelationSketch calls handleRelationQueryForPattern(exchange, lemma, relation, RelationType.SURFACE); handleFullDependencySketch calls handleDepSketch(exchange, lemma) — none add auth, logging, metrics, or caching

GrammarConfigLoader, HttpApiUtils, RelationConfig, ExplorationResult all carry multi-sentence design-justification prose in class/method Javadoc ('Why a class rather than a record', 'Nullability design note', 10-line justification for co-locating two concerns) — these explain intent rather than restate code, so most are borderline, but the volume is heavier than typical domain code
No restating comments (// increment counter above i++) found across the seed files
SLF4J used at appropriate levels; no entry/exit trace logging or full-object console dumps found
Domain-specific names used throughout: SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator, RelationPatternBuilder, logDice — no handleData/processItem generics

RelationType enum (model/RelationType.java) contains both new structural types (SURFACE, DEP) and four old semantic-specific types (ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF). The grammar config (grammars/relations.json) assigns only SURFACE or DEP to every relation — none use the old semantic types.

CollocateProfileComparator passes RelationType.ADJ_MODIFIER and RelationType.ADJ_PREDICATE as primaryTypes to RelationUtils.findBestCollocatePattern; SemanticFieldExplorer passes RelationType.OBJECT_OF and RelationType.SUBJECT_OF. Because no grammar relation carries these types, the primary-filter stream always finds zero matches and silently falls through to the generic fallback.

WordSketchApiServer registers /api/semantic-field as a live GET handler, marked with a block comment: '/* @deprecated legacy alias for /api/semantic-field/compare; kept for backward compatibility. Use /api/semantic-field/compare instead.' No removal timeline or deprecation annotation is present.

GrammarConfigLoader.validateNoLegacyKeys actively rejects any grammar config that still contains a 'copulas' top-level key, demonstrating that a prior migration (embedding copula lemmas in CQL patterns rather than a separate list) was completed and enforced.
model.QueryResults is imported consistently by all callers (api, exploration, query packages) — a prior package migration from query.QueryResults to model.QueryResults appears fully complete.

Dependency direction is strictly layered: api→{config,model,exploration,query,viz,utils}, exploration→{config,model,query}, config→{model,utils}, query→{model,utils}, model→(nothing). No bidirectional edges found.
Package names (api, config, exploration, indexer, model, query, utils) accurately describe contained responsibilities with no ambiguity.

model/ package has 15 files (including package-info.java) — two distinct sub-clusters live together: query-layer types (QueryResults, FetchExamplesOptions, PosGroup, RelationType, RelationEdgeType) consumed by query/ and config/, and exploration-domain types (ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult, AdjectiveProfile, CoreCollocate, DiscoveredNoun, SharingCategory, Edge) consumed primarily by exploration/ and api/.
viz/ is a single-file package (RadialPlot.java, 242 LOC) with exactly one consumer: api/VisualizationHandlers.java. Import affinity is 100% from api/.
api/ has 10 files (1567 LOC) all within HTTP-handling concerns: 5 route-handler classes, 1 server, 1 HTTP utility, 1 exception, 1 serializer, 1 assembler — cohesive despite file count.
Main.java is a genuine entry-point root file with cross-package imports spanning api, config, indexer, query — correctly not flagged as a straggler.
package_organization.issues_preventing_higher_score: Two structural loose ends prevent a higher score: (1) viz/ is a one-file package whose sole consumer is api/, making it an isolated thin module with no isolation benefit; (2) model/ accumulates 15 files blending query-layer and exploration-domain types without sub-package differentiation, obscuring ownership boundaries for future contributors.

evidence: QueryExecutor interface documents each method's null/empty-input behaviour, return semantics, and exception contracts in detail — e.g., executeCollocations says 'returns an empty list silently if null or empty' and documents the cqlPattern format requirement with an IllegalArgumentException note.

HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling documents exactly which exception types map to which HTTP status codes, and every public request-parsing method (requireParam, parseIntParam, parseDoubleParam) documents its error contract (throws IAE, caught by the wrapper).
@NonNull annotation on parseSeedSet(String) parameter contradicts the immediate null guard 'if (seedsParam == null || seedsParam.isEmpty())' inside the method body — one of the two is wrong.

SemanticFieldExplorer.compareCollocateProfiles takes @NonNull Set<String> seedNouns and validates only size<2; its delegate CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles (no @NonNull on the same param) additionally checks 'if (seedNouns == null...)' — the two layers express inconsistent nullability contracts for the same parameter.

ConlluConverter.convertConlluToWplChunks returns long[] with positional semantics conveyed only through comments in the caller ('counts[0], counts[1], counts[2]') and private class-level index constants (STATE_SENTENCES=0, etc.) invisible to callers — the return type does not encode its structure.

CollocateQueryHelper constructor Javadoc says null index 'is accepted only in test subclasses that override getTotalFrequency and executeCollocateSearchWithContent', but the enforcement is deferred to Objects.requireNonNull inside those methods rather than the constructor itself; the contract is accurate but relies on indirection.
GrammarConfig.relation(String id) returns Optional<RelationConfig> which accurately models the absent-relation case; all callers in the codebase handle both present and empty branches correctly.
Guard clauses are correctly placed before processing (e.g., null/empty lemma guards in executeCollocations and executeSurfacePattern return early before any computation)
Multi-phase algorithms (exploration, BCQL query) are decomposed into helper methods with single responsibilities, making control flow easy to follow and verify

In ExploreResponseAssembler.buildExplorationEdges, the outer loop iterates result.seedCollocates() which in multi-seed mode is the UNION of ALL seeds' collocates (merged via Math::max in MultiSeedExplorer.findCollocateIntersection), then the inner loop creates one SEED_ADJ edge per seed for every collocate — producing a full cross-product. Collocates belonging to only one seed generate spurious edges to all other seeds with incorrect max-logDice weights.

ExplorationHandlers.parseSeedSet is annotated @NonNull on its parameter, yet the method immediately checks `if (seedsParam == null || seedsParam.isEmpty())` — the null branch is unreachable under the declared contract, sending mixed signals to callers and readers about whether null is expected.

JSpecify @Nullable/@NonNull annotations used on constructor parameters, return types, and method parameters throughout (e.g., CollocateQueryHelper(@Nullable BlackLabIndex), QueryExecutor.executeCollocations(@Nullable String lemma), BlackLabSnippetParser.extractLemmaFromMatch returns @Nullable String)
Sealed interface ConcordanceResult with permits SnippetResult, CollocateResult provides exhaustive subtype tracking at compile time
RelationType, PosGroup, SharingCategory, RelationEdgeType are proper enums used consistently for domain classification
Optional<RelationType> used for optional enum fields in RelationConfig with correct orElseThrow()/orElse() call sites

RelationConfig.name and RelationConfig.description record fields are unannotated (@Nullable absent) despite GrammarConfigSerializer guarding against null: 'if (rel.name() != null) obj.put(...)' — type contract not expressed at the field level

ExplorationHandlers calls .relationType().orElseThrow().name() to extract a raw String relationType which is then passed to buildCoreExploreResponse(String relationType, ...) — the enum is widened to String one method too early; the response-assembly helper accepts String instead of RelationType

VisualizationHandlers uses a raw String comparison 'mode.equals("signed")' with no enum; the only valid non-null mode value implies a one-value domain that was never formalised as an enum or a validated type

parseSeedSet(@NonNull String seedsParam) immediately checks 'if (seedsParam == null || ...)' — the @NonNull annotation and the null guard contradict each other; the annotation signals callers not to pass null but the guard silently accepts it
56 occurrences of Map<String, Object> in the api package for response building — response shape is untyped and verified only at runtime by the JSON serialiser

CollocateQueryHelper constructor accepts @Nullable BlackLabIndex with a runtime Objects.requireNonNull guard — the Javadoc carves out a 'test subclass' exemption but the annotation widens the visible contract to all callers

ExploreResponseAssembler.populateComparisonResponse (lines 168-196) constructs the full response envelope — status, seeds, seed_count, and parameters — internally, while its sibling populateExploreResponse (lines 72-87) only appends domain-specific fields (seed_collocates, discovered_nouns, core_collocates, edges) to a response map that the handler already seeded via buildCoreExploreResponse. This gives the two 'populate*' methods inconsistent scopes within the same class.

The comparison handler (ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison, lines 155-176) creates an empty HashMap and hands it entirely to populateComparisonResponse, which then writes status, seeds, parameters, adjectives, counts and edges. The explore/explore-multi handlers, by contrast, call buildCoreExploreResponse first, add seed-specific fields, then call populateExploreResponse only for domain data. The handler-assembler responsibility split differs between the two paths.

populateComparisonResponse accepts a `relation` parameter (line 169) that is always called with the string literal 'all' from the handler. The parameter looks like it could vary, but the comparison path has no legitimate variation: CollocateProfileComparator always aggregates across all relations. A caller who passes anything other than 'all' would produce misleading API responses with no failure signal.
Dependency direction is cleanly layered: api → exploration → query → model, with no upward cycles detected in production code
QueryExecutor interface in query package acts as a proper seam: exploration and api both depend on the interface, not on BlackLabQueryExecutor directly
Handler classes in api package are package-private (class Foo vs public class Foo), correctly containing the api surface area
model package has zero cross-package imports — pure data layer with no upward dependencies
MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator are package-private within exploration, correctly narrowing the visible surface of that subsystem
GrammarConfigSerializer is public and lives in api but is imported by GrammarConfigLoaderTest in the config test package, creating an upward config-test → api-production boundary violation

MAX_REQUEST_BODY_BYTES = 65536 is independently defined in both CorpusQueryHandlers and VisualizationHandlers — the natural shared home (HttpApiUtils) already owns readBodyWithSizeLimit() but not the constant

Main.java uses the concrete BlackLabQueryExecutor type directly in handleBlackLabQueryCommand (try (BlackLabQueryExecutor executor = ...)) while handleBlackLabServerCommand correctly uses QueryExecutor executor = new BlackLabQueryExecutor(...)
No static initializer blocks anywhere in the production code; all state is instance-level.

BlackLabQueryExecutor opens the index in its constructor but declares `throws IOException`, includes a Javadoc rationale, and is explicitly constructed by callers — not lazily or via a hidden singleton.

WordSketchApiServer separates construction from port binding: handler objects are wired in the constructor, the TCP socket is only bound when `start()` is called, and the Javadoc documents this as an intentional design choice.

HttpApiUtils.setCorsHeader reads `cors.allow.origin` via `System.getProperty` at request-handler call time (not at class load time), explicitly to allow per-test overrides — documented in the Javadoc.

GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() reads `System.getProperty("grammar.config", "grammars/relations.json")` at call time; tests in RelationsValidationTest and HybridAlgorithmTest call it directly, creating an implicit dependency on the working directory being the project root.
Main.java reads `System.getProperty("grammar.config", ...)` inline in the command handler and is not affected by `createDefaultEnglish()` — the two call-sites are independent and consistent.
AtomicReference<WordSketchApiServer> is used in the shutdown hook to safely bridge the gap between hook registration and server construction — a deliberate, correct treatment of the init race.
36 test files cover all major handler, exploration, query helper, config, and utility classes via stub-based unit tests
StubQueryExecutor is a well-designed shared test double reused across 10+ test classes — no coupling to real BlackLab I/O

5 test files carry @Disabled at class or nested-class level: BlackLabConllUIndexerTest, BlackLabBcqlConcordanceTest, BlackLabQueryExecutorIntegrationTest, DependencySketchTest, and the LiveIndex nested class in BlackLabQueryExecutorTest

RelationPatternBuilder (used by SketchHandlers.routeSketchRequest and ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples) has no direct test; handler tests inject a StubQueryExecutor so the built BCQL pattern is never executed or inspected
No timing dependencies, no snapshot tests, no external-state fragility detected in any runnable test
Full-sketch endpoint (handleFullSketchForType) emits per-relation maps with {name, cql, collocate_pos_group, collocations} — no 'id', no 'total_matches'
Single-relation endpoint (handleRelationQueryForPattern) emits {id, name, collocations, total_matches} — no 'cql', no 'collocate_pos_group'
Dep-sketch (buildRelationResponse) emits {id, name, description, deprel, total_matches, collocations} — a third, partially overlapping shape

In explore/explore-multi, 'relation' is echoed inside 'parameters' as the enum type name (e.g. 'SURFACE'); in /api/concordance/examples it is echoed at the top level as the relation ID (e.g. 'noun_adj_predicates')
wrapWithErrorHandling maps IAE→400 and IOException→500 uniformly across all handlers; requireParam/parseIntParam/parseDoubleParam used consistently; 'status: ok' present in every success response
WordSketchApiServer Javadoc explicitly documents the security model: loopback-only bind (127.0.0.1), all endpoints unauthenticated, access control delegated to host firewall.
All 14 routes are registered through the same registerGetHandler/registerPostHandler helpers — no sibling receives different auth treatment.
CORS is applied uniformly via setCorsHeader() called by every response-sending method (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendBinaryResponse, sendOptionsResponse).

HttpApiUtils.requireParam enforces MAX_PARAM_LENGTH (200 chars) on all GET query parameters uniformly; CorpusQueryHandlers additionally enforces body size (65 536 bytes) and pattern complexity (20 bracket tokens) for the POST /api/bcql endpoint — appropriate per request type.
The cors.allow.origin=* case logs a warning but does not prevent the widened trust boundary from being applied silently at startup.
No permission strings, no roles, no auth decorators exist anywhere in the codebase — consistent with the documented all-public design.
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: The CORS wildcard (cors.allow.origin=*) is accepted at runtime with only a log warning — no startup failure or hard guard prevents accidentally widening the browser trust boundary across all endpoints in a containerised deployment. A single startup check would close this gap.

strengths: Package decomposition maps one-to-one to named domain capabilities (api, config, exploration, model, query, utils, viz, indexer) with no folder whose existence would puzzle a newcomer.
GrammarConfig / GrammarConfigLoader split cleanly separates the immutable data object from the file-I/O machinery, and both types document the rationale for the split.
model/package-info.java provides an explicit boundary contract stating which type categories belong here and forbidding I/O concerns — a rare and valuable ownership anchor.

API handler classes (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers) each own one coherent HTTP concern and are wired together only at the top-level WordSketchApiServer.
Options-object types (ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions) apply a single consistent pattern across all entry points for extensible parameter passing.
high_level_elegance.issue_character: The issues are localised to one enum type and two stale javadoc cross-references: not a systemic structural failure, but the RelationType enum conflation is a real ownership blurring that a new engineer would notice immediately when tracing how exploration and sketching routes use the same enum for different purposes.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The package structure, module separation, and documented boundaries are well above average — this codebase would score near 85 if the architecture fully matched its stated contracts. Two concrete mismatch points pull the score down: the RelationType enum conflates dispatch strategy with semantic role labels (blurring why SURFACE/DEP exist alongside ADJ_PREDICATE etc.), and RelationConfig's javadoc falsely claims deriveDeprel() has no outside callers while SketchHandlers calls it in three places. These are real trust-eroding documentation-to-reality gaps, not mere style nits, bringing the score to the 70-75 range — solid decomposition, mostly trustworthy docs, but with a notable enum ownership confusion and two explicit doc/reality mismatches.

strengths: Handler class structure in the api package is remarkably uniform: all five sibling handler classes are package-private, follow the same constructor injection pattern, and each uses a private inner record to group request parameters — no handler is a structural outlier.
Naming conventions are perfectly consistent across the entire codebase: PascalCase classes, camelCase methods, SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE constants, with no snake_case drift or mixed conventions anywhere.

The model package's deliberate mix of records and classes is explicitly documented in both the class Javadoc (ExplorationResult explains its rationale) and in model/package-info.java, making the intentional variation visible and justified rather than silent drift.
Handler public method name prefixes (handle*, route*, parse*) follow a consistent verb-based naming scheme across all sibling classes.
convention_outlier.issue_character: Issues are isolated and narrow: one @NonNull annotation that contradicts a live null check in the same method, and one documentation convention (package-info.java) applied to only one of seven packages. There is no systemic naming drift, no style islands, and no structural outliers among sibling modules.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase scores solidly on convention_outlier. The api package handler protocol is one of the most consistently structured sibling-module patterns observable — every handler follows the same construction, visibility, and naming conventions without exception. The only concrete outliers are the @NonNull-plus-null-check contradiction in parseSeedSet (a single method with two conflicting signals) and the one-of-seven package-info.java pattern (a documentation convention inconsistently applied). These are small isolated issues; there are no style islands, no naming drift, and no mixed export or type-strategy outliers without documented rationale. An 83 reflects strong consistency with two minor, localized deviations that a developer would notice but not be blocked by.

strengths: wrapWithErrorHandling provides a single, uniform HTTP error boundary applied to every registered route — developers can predict exactly how any exception becomes an HTTP status code
Handler layer is exception-only with no null returns; IllegalArgumentException signals client errors, IOException signals server errors — clean two-exception model across the entire API surface
BlackLabSnippetParser's null-returning parse utilities are all annotated @Nullable, making the optional-result contract explicit and consistent across that class
InvalidQuery (a BlackLab RuntimeException) is systematically converted to IOException at the executor boundary so callers never need to know about library-internal exception types

Partial-failure in multi-relation sketch requests is handled deliberately: per-relation IOExceptions are caught, logged, and forwarded to the caller as a response.errors field rather than silently discarded
error_consistency.issue_character: The single inconsistency is narrow and local: CLI argument parsing in Main uses bare parseDouble/parseInt without NumberFormatException handling, producing a generic error message where the API layer would produce a clear IAE, but nothing is silently swallowed.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The API layer has exemplary error consistency — a single well-tested boundary handler, uniform exception semantics across all handlers, and honest @Nullable contracts in parsing utilities. The codebase scores solidly above 80. The only friction is in the CLI path where two numeric argument parses (--port, --min-logdice) lack the NumberFormatException-to-IAE wrapping that the HTTP layer applies, leaving the user with a less actionable error message; this is a local, fixable gap that keeps the score from reaching 90.

strengths: Class and interface names are domain-precise and immediately communicate role: SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator, MultiSeedExplorer, BlackLabSnippetParser each name the exact operation performed.
Handler method names form a coherent handle<Domain><Concept> convention, making it straightforward to locate a handler from an endpoint path.
Constants are fully self-documenting (DEFAULT_SKETCH_RESULTS, SINGLE_RELATION_RESULTS, OVER_FETCH_FACTOR) — no magic numbers in the scoring or result-limiting logic.
Parameter names across the API and domain layers consistently use corpus-linguistics vocabulary: seed, collocate, lemma, minLogDice, topCollocates, relationType.
Private records used as parameter bundles (ExplorationParams, ExecutedSketch, CollocateSearch, HitRecord) all name their purpose precisely and avoid generic containers.
naming_quality.issue_character: The issues are isolated pockets of abbreviation drift and vocabulary mismatch rather than a systemic pattern: a handful of 'colloc' shorthands against a 'collocate' norm, one record that renames 'seed'/'collocate' to 'noun'/'adjective', and a cluster of thin wrapper methods in SketchHandlers whose name-change indirection adds navigational friction without expressing any new distinction.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase sits clearly above the 'significant drag' threshold (60): class names, constants, and public API surfaces are domain-aligned and precise, making the codebase easy to navigate at a high level. What pulls it below 'solid but uneven' (80) is the small but genuine vocabulary inconsistency around 'colloc' vs 'collocate', the noun/adjective renaming in ConcordanceExamplesRequest that diverges from system-wide terms, and the thin-wrapper naming pattern in SketchHandlers that forces unnecessary name-change hops. These are real friction points for developers tracing execution, but they are bounded to a few files and methods rather than reflecting a codebase-wide naming philosophy problem.

strengths: ExploreResponseAssembler is a genuine translation layer: it builds edge lists, formats collocate maps, and serialises model objects, giving callers a clean presentation contract without any logic leaking into handler classes.
RelationPatternBuilder and GrammarConfigSerializer successfully keep external library dependencies (BlackLab pattern syntax, fastjson2) out of core data records, a clear win for the config layer.
The HTTP handler decomposition (five distinct handler classes) is domain-driven, not arbitrary: each class owns a coherent slice of the API surface and has real independent logic.
HttpApiUtils carries genuine cross-cutting policy: CORS enforcement, request size limits, method checking, and error-to-HTTP-status mapping — it is not a mere static utility dump.
CollocateQueryHelper encapsulates non-trivial collocate ranking, over-fetch logic, and logDice scoring that would pollute BlackLabQueryExecutor if inlined.
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: Issues are isolated to one facade class (SemanticFieldExplorer) whose two delegation methods add a call-depth hop with no behavior, plus minor one-liner private aliases in SketchHandlers — a thin-facade pattern concentrated in the exploration subsystem rather than a systemic codebase problem.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: The codebase earns a solid foundation: most abstractions carry real behavioral weight, and the design comments show deliberate intent. Two identifiable pass-through patterns pull the score down from 'solid but uneven' (80): SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreMultiSeed and compareCollocateProfiles are self-admitted thin delegations that unpack an options record and immediately hand off, and the QueryExecutor interface has a single production implementation (acknowledged as a testability seam). These are real but bounded — they affect the exploration subsystem and one interface, not the full codebase. The score lands at 74: meaningfully above 'significant drag' (60) because most abstractions pay their way, but below 'solid' (80) due to the concentrated thin-facade pattern in a key subsystem.

strengths: Dependency set is minimal and purposeful: only 5 runtime deps, all substantively used across multiple files
Single JSON library (fastjson2) used consistently and heavily across the entire API layer — no competing JSON parsers
SLF4J + Logback are correctly version-paired (both 2.x compatible), with no apparent transitive version conflict
jspecify used as a lightweight annotation-only dependency across 17 files rather than pulling in a heavier nullability framework
Test dependencies (JUnit Jupiter, JUnit Platform) are correctly scoped to 'test', keeping the production classpath clean
dependency_health.issue_character: The single substantive issue is a fragile external repository URL for a critical dependency rather than any classic dependency misuse pattern — no redundancy, bloat, or version conflicts are present.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The dependency set is lean and clean: every declared dependency is directly used in multiple production files, no libraries are duplicated for the same purpose, and version pairings are correct. The sole weakness is that the core BlackLab dependency (4.0.0) is resolved from a raw GitHub raw-content URL with no SLA, which is a real but acknowledged build-reliability risk. This pulls the score below 'solid' (80) but not into significant drag territory (60), landing around 74 — a well-curated dependency set with one supply-chain fragility that has been identified but not mitigated.

strengths: Phase-structured algorithms in SemanticFieldExplorer and CollocateQueryHelper: each phase has its own method with a clear single responsibility, making the multi-step algorithms readable
Consistent guard-clause style across all API and query methods — early returns on null/empty inputs prevent deep nesting
Regex patterns in BlackLabSnippetParser are pre-compiled at class level, not allocated per call — a deliberate, correct performance decision
Helper extraction is coherent and consistent: CollocateQueryHelper, RelationPatternBuilder, ExploreResponseAssembler each own exactly one concern without leaking into each other
ConlluConverter cleanly separates chunk-rotation (rotateChunkIfNeeded) from line-processing (processLines), with documented invariants
low_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are localized to three independent spots: a responsibility mismatch in BlackLabSnippetParser, a primitive mutable-accumulator pattern in ConlluConverter, and a minor annotation/guard contradiction in ExplorationHandlers — none of these are systemic patterns that pervade the codebase.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase is well above the 60 threshold: control flow is direct, extraction level is balanced, and helper extraction is consistent across the main subsystems. What pulls the score below 80 are the BlackLabSnippetParser responsibility leak (BCQL pattern analysis sitting in an XML-parsing class), the ConlluConverter state-array idiom that requires the caller to interpret null returns and manually reset local variables, and the callback-heavy executeRelationQueries design that obscures flow for limited benefit. These are real readability costs for a developer navigating the code, but they are isolated rather than forming a pattern, placing this firmly in the 70–75 range.

strengths: ExploreResponseAssembler centralises all model-to-presentation translation for exploration results, keeping HTTP handler classes free of serialisation logic
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling provides a single, consistent error boundary across every registered endpoint, mapping IllegalArgumentException→400 and IOException→500 uniformly
Handler classes (ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers) receive typed collaborators via constructor injection, making cross-layer dependencies explicit and testable
SemanticFieldExplorer acts as a stable facade that shields HTTP handlers from algorithm-level delegation changes (MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator)
GrammarConfigLoader separates file-IO from parsing with a fromReader factory, giving callers a clean testability seam independent of the file system
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Issues are concentrated at two specific translation seams: the model→graph edge-building seam (where per-seed attribution is lost during aggregation) and two places where boundary classification or parameter decomposition is duplicated rather than delegated to the owning type.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase achieves solid layering overall: handler/assembler/explorer/executor boundaries are explicit, error propagation is consistent, and the assembler pattern correctly removes serialisation from domain objects. The score is pulled down by one genuine data-accuracy defect at the model→presentation seam (multi-seed edge cross-product) and two smaller duplications of boundary logic that create divergence risk. These sit below the 80 'solid but uneven' anchor because the edge defect is not cosmetic — it silently inflates the graph with edges that were never observed. Overall quality sits around the low 70s: better than 'significant drag at 60' (the seams are well-structured and consistent) but not yet 'solid' because the primary translation seam has a correctness gap.

strengths: No restating comments anywhere in the seed files — comments consistently explain design rationale, algorithm intent, or non-obvious constraints rather than echoing the code
SLF4J used at appropriate levels with structured arguments; no debug entry/exit logging spam or System.out object dumps

Domain terminology used consistently for class and method names (SemanticFieldExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator, RelationPatternBuilder, logDice) — no generic handleData or processItem placeholders
RequestEntityTooLargeException, Edge, RelationEdgeType — trivial types have proportionally brief Javadoc; elaboration is reserved for genuinely complex types
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: The debt is isolated to a single subsystem (SketchHandlers): three private single-line pass-through delegates that add no logic and could be inlined at their single call sites in routeSketchRequest.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase scores well on most AI-generated-debt signals: comments explain WHY not WHAT, naming is domain-specific, and there is no debug-logging or null-check overengineering. The one clear pattern is three pass-through wrappers (handleFullSketch, handleRelationSketch, handleFullDependencySketch) in SketchHandlers that each forward to a more specific method with no added logic — a classic AI refactoring artefact. Individually trivial, but as a cluster of three they represent a distinct naming-layer habit that harms readability. Score sits at 82: solid overall, pulled down from the high 80s by the concentrated pass-through cluster.

strengths: The QueryResults type migration from the query package to the model package is fully complete: all production callers import pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.model.QueryResults without a single stale reference.
GrammarConfigLoader actively enforces a completed migration by throwing on the old 'copulas' config key, preventing any regression to the prior configuration style.
The deprecated /api/semantic-field endpoint is clearly documented with a block comment naming the canonical replacement and logs a warn-level message on every invocation, giving operators visibility.
incomplete_migration.issue_character: The incomplete migration issues are concentrated in one enum (RelationType) whose four old semantic-category constants are vestigial: they coexist with the current SURFACE/DEP taxonomy but are never populated by any grammar config, so any code that passes them as priority hints silently falls back to generic matching — a subtle behavioural gap rather than a crash.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase sits at roughly 76/100 for this dimension. Two completed migrations (QueryResults package move, copulas config removal) and one well-marked compatibility shim (legacy /api/semantic-field endpoint) push the score well above the 60 threshold. What holds it below 80 is the stale RelationType enum values: four dead constants are still passed as meaningful priority hints in CollocateProfileComparator and SemanticFieldExplorer, making the primary-type filter always fall through without any log or comment to explain this, which is a hidden behavioural inconsistency. The legacy endpoint also lacks any removal timeline, leaving it as open-ended debt.

strengths: Dependency graph is strictly directed: api at the top, model/utils at the bottom, no cycles or bidirectional edges observed anywhere.
Package names accurately reflect the responsibilities they contain (config, exploration, query, indexer) — no ambiguous or misleading folder names.
model/ package includes a documented package-info.java explicitly stating its boundary: no persistence, I/O, or HTTP concerns belong here.
api/ package cohesively groups all HTTP concerns (handlers, server, utilities, serializers, assemblers) without leaking presentation logic into domain packages.
config/ cleanly separates grammar data (GrammarConfig, RelationConfig) from loading logic (GrammarConfigLoader) and utilities (RelationPatternBuilder, RelationUtils).
package_organization.issue_character: Issues are isolated and localized — a thin single-file viz/ package with misaligned affinity and a slightly overloaded model/ that conflates two domain clusters — rather than systemic coupling or boundary failures across the codebase.
package_organization.score_rationale: The codebase's layered dependency structure, accurate package naming, and clear separation of HTTP/domain/config/query concerns place it solidly above the 'significant drag' threshold. The two issues (viz/ isolation and model/ cluster mixing) are real organizational loose ends that add navigational cost for new contributors but do not represent deep structural problems or coupling failures. This sits in the 'solid but uneven' band — clear strengths in the overall architecture, with specific sub-package granularity left to mature.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface Javadoc specifies null/empty input handling, return semantics, and exception contracts for every method — a strong model for the rest of the codebase.
HttpApiUtils.wrapWithErrorHandling explicitly maps each exception type to an HTTP status code and is referenced by name in each method that relies on it, making the error contract traceable.
GrammarConfig.relation() returns Optional<RelationConfig> and is used consistently with orElse/orElseThrow throughout the API layer — no silent null propagation.
@NonNull/@Nullable annotations on method returns and parameters are applied consistently in the exploration and query layers, especially on public facade methods of SemanticFieldExplorer.
contract_coherence.issue_character: The issues are isolated annotation/guard inconsistencies at module boundaries rather than systemic contract failures; no method lies about its return type or silently swallows exceptions it claims to throw.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase scores solidly above the midpoint. Contracts are well-documented throughout the QueryExecutor interface and the HTTP utility layer, error propagation is consistent and traceable, and Optional is used correctly where absence is the normal case. What prevents a higher score is a small cluster of annotation-vs-implementation contradictions: the @NonNull param that has a redundant null guard, the split nullability contract between SemanticFieldExplorer and its delegate CollocateProfileComparator, and the untyped long[] return from ConlluConverter whose positional semantics are invisible to callers. These are isolated module-boundary issues, not deep structural problems, placing the score around 79 — solid but uneven at the edges.

strengths: Guard clauses are consistently placed before computation: null/empty lemma checks in executeCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, and executeDependencyPattern all return empty lists before touching the index
Boolean conditions are direct and non-redundant throughout — no identical if/else branches, no always-true conditions, no `if x return true else return false` patterns were found
The two-phase BCQL hit-collection pipeline (collectHits → scoreHits) separates concerns cleanly and each conditional inside (null concordance, null parts, empty collocate) has distinct semantics
Exception routing is logically consistent: IllegalArgumentException for client validation errors (caught as 400), IOException for index failures (caught as 500), with no overlapping catch blocks
logic_clarity.issue_character: Issues are isolated rather than systemic: one semantic logic error in multi-seed edge construction (cross-product vs per-seed actual edges) and one minor annotation-versus-implementation inconsistency; they do not reflect a broad pattern of unclear or incorrect logic.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The codebase handles control flow correctly and clearly across nearly all paths — guard clauses are well-placed, conditions are not redundant, and multi-step algorithms decompose into verifiable phases. The main substantive defect is in ExploreResponseAssembler.buildExplorationEdges where multi-seed mode produces SEED_ADJ edges connecting every seed to every collocate in the union set, regardless of actual per-seed association; this makes the multi-seed graph semantically incorrect (misrepresented edges with max-of-all-seeds logDice weights). The defect is confined to one method and one code path, but it silently produces wrong output for a featured use-case. This pulls the score below the 'solid but uneven' 80 mark while the otherwise clean logic holds it well above the 60 'significant drag' threshold.

strengths: JSpecify @Nullable/@NonNull annotations present on all major public API boundaries, giving callers nullability contracts for key methods
Sealed ConcordanceResult interface with permits clause gives the compiler exhaustive knowledge of all subtype variants
Domain concepts (RelationType, PosGroup, SharingCategory) modelled as enums throughout; no raw-string comparisons for enum-like state except the isolated mode case
Optional<RelationType> used correctly in RelationConfig to model the absent case, with disciplined orElseThrow()/orElse() call sites
Records used for DTOs (WordSketchResult, CollocateResult, SnippetResult, HitRecord, SeedCollocateData) eliminating mutable state and providing predictable field types
type_safety.issue_character: The issues are mild and largely structural: enum values are widened to raw Strings one level before serialisation, a handful of record fields lack @Nullable despite defensive null guards at their use sites, and the HTTP response layer uniformly uses Map<String, Object> rather than typed response shapes — a pattern that is repeated across all six handler classes.
type_safety.score_rationale: The codebase scores well above average on Java type safety fundamentals: JSpecify is in use, enums are the norm, sealed types are exploited correctly, and Optional is used idiomatically. What pulls the score below 80 is a consistent pattern in the API layer where enum values are prematurely stringified (RelationType.name() passed as String through a method boundary), where Map<String, Object> provides no structural guarantee across 56 response-building call sites, and where several RelationConfig record fields carry no nullability annotation despite defensive null checks at serialisation time. These are all real but fixable type-discipline gaps, not deep design flaws — comparable to a codebase at 80=solid but uneven where the unevenness is concentrated in one layer.

strengths: ExplorationParams record (ExplorationHandlers.java line 243) cleanly bundles the four HTTP parameters parsed from every explore request, eliminating four-arg passing chains across the handler methods.

SemanticFieldExplorer follows an explicit facade pattern: it owns the single-seed algorithm directly and delegates multi-seed and comparison to dedicated classes (MultiSeedExplorer, CollocateProfileComparator), giving each algorithm a focused owner.

The four phase-helper methods in SemanticFieldExplorer (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) each have a single well-named responsibility and compose into a readable pipeline.
Handler methods in ExplorationHandlers are tightly scoped — each parses params, builds typed options, calls domain, assembles response, and sends — with no leaking of HTTP concerns into domain calls.
design_coherence.issue_character: The single genuine design coherence issue is a bounded but real inconsistency in how ExploreResponseAssembler partitions responsibilities between the explore and comparison paths — the comparison assembler owns envelope construction that should belong to the handler, mirroring what the explore path already does correctly.
design_coherence.score_rationale: The codebase shows clear, deliberate design thinking across most of its surface: focused handler methods, earned abstractions, the facade in SemanticFieldExplorer, and the ExplorationParams record all reflect consistent structural discipline. One path — the comparison response assembly — breaks the handler/assembler boundary that the rest of the code follows, putting envelope construction (status, seeds, parameters) inside the assembler and accepting a nominally variable `relation` parameter that is always 'all'. This is a real inconsistency, not a stylistic preference, and it will cause confusion for any developer extending the comparison path. Anchored against 'solid but uneven' at 80: the issue is confined but the boundary confusion is exactly the kind of friction the design_coherence dimension measures.

strengths: Layered dependency direction (api → exploration → query → model) is clean with no production cycles detected
QueryExecutor interface provides a genuine architectural seam between the api/exploration subsystems and the BlackLab implementation, enabling independent testability
Handler classes are package-private throughout the api package — the boundary is enforced at the language level, not just by convention
model package is a clean leaf node with no cross-package dependencies, serving all higher layers without pulling anything back
MultiSeedExplorer and CollocateProfileComparator are package-private within exploration — internal collaborators are invisible to callers outside the subsystem
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: Issues are concentrated at boundary edges rather than in the core dependency graph: a serializer misplaced in the wrong layer that forces a test in a lower layer to reach upward, a duplicated constant that should be centralised in the package utility class, and an inconsistency in the composition root between interface and concrete type usage.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The primary dependency graph is well-structured — layers flow in one direction, interfaces are used as seams, and internal helpers are correctly scoped as package-private. What pulls the score below 80 is the GrammarConfigSerializer boundary violation: a config-layer test must import from the api layer to exercise config serialization, meaning the serializer is housed in the wrong layer and the boundary between config and api is partially inverted. The duplicated MAX_REQUEST_BODY_BYTES constant and the inconsistent concrete-vs-interface usage in Main.java are lesser issues but confirm that the api package's internal boundary discipline is uneven. On a 100-point scale this is solidly functional architecture (above 60) but with identifiable, fixable boundary drift rather than exemplary discipline (80+).

strengths: No static mutable global state or singletons anywhere — all shared objects are constructed explicitly and passed via constructors.
No import-time or class-load-time side effects: there are no static initializer blocks, no eager DB/file/network operations at class load.
Constructor I/O in BlackLabQueryExecutor is honest (declared `throws IOException`, documented with rationale) rather than hidden behind a lazy-init singleton.
Port binding is cleanly separated from object construction in WordSketchApiServer, with a clear Javadoc note explaining why, avoiding surprise side effects on construction.
CORS system-property is read per-request rather than cached at class load, explicitly designed to allow test overrides.
initialization_coupling.issue_character: The single identifiable fragility is localized to one factory method (`createDefaultEnglish`) that silently depends on a working-directory-relative file path resolved at call time via a system property; test code calls it directly without any parameter injection, coupling tests to the project-root file layout.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: The codebase is largely exemplary on this dimension: explicit construction, no hidden singletons, no static mutable state, and documented design decisions around constructor I/O and deferred port binding. The one real concern — `createDefaultEnglish()` reading a system property and being called directly in tests — is a mild fragility that would cause subtle failures if tests ran from a different working directory, but the default is sensible and the override mechanism is documented. This places the codebase solidly in the 85-90 range: strong discipline with one low-severity, localized issue.

strengths: Comprehensive stub-based handler tests cover happy-path and all significant validation branches for every HTTP handler (ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers)
StubQueryExecutor is a clean, shared test double that decouples all unit tests from the real BlackLab index — no test fragility or ordering issues
SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, and CollocateProfileComparator each have dedicated unit tests exercising meaningful business logic with canned data
Live-index tests are disabled with clear documented runbook (CONCEPT_SKETCH_TEST_INDEX env var + remove @Disabled) rather than silently skipped or deleted
No snapshot overuse, no timing dependencies, no random seeds — the runnable test suite is deterministic and stable
test_strategy.issue_character: The gaps are concentrated at two boundaries: (1) the BCQL pattern-building layer (RelationPatternBuilder) is exercised by handler tests only through a stub that ignores the built pattern, and (2) the entire indexing pipeline and live-query execution path has zero CI-runnable tests, leaving a significant chunk of production code unvalidated by any automated run.
test_strategy.score_rationale: The runnable test portfolio is solid and well-structured for a corpus-analysis tool that inherently depends on an external index: handler coverage, exploration logic, config loading, and utilities are all tested. This lifts the score above 60. The two structural gaps — RelationPatternBuilder's pattern substitution logic is never actually executed in tests, and the whole BlackLab I/O layer (indexer + executor) has no CI path — mean a developer making changes in those areas cannot rely on the test suite for feedback, which is the definition of the 70–80 band: 'a developer would worry about making changes in certain areas'.

strengths: Uniform error contract: wrapWithErrorHandling centralises IAE→400, IOE→500, unexpected→500 across every registered endpoint
Parameter parsing infrastructure (requireParam, parseIntParam, parseDoubleParam, parseQueryParams) is used consistently in every handler — no ad-hoc parsing
'status: ok' is present in every JSON success response across all handlers
Sibling endpoints /api/semantic-field/examples and /api/concordance/examples mirror each other using the same 'seed'/'collocate'/'top' parameter names
POST-only constraint is applied consistently to both /api/bcql and /api/visual/radial, with matching enforcement via registerPostHandler
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The inconsistencies are not random noise but structural: the three sketch endpoint variants each return a different per-relation response shape, and the 'relation' echo appears at different nesting levels with different value types across endpoints — a developer must read each implementation to know what fields to expect.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The cross-cutting concerns (error handling, param parsing, status fields) are executed with high consistency, which places the baseline comfortably above 60. However, the per-relation response shape divergence between full-sketch, single-relation, and dep-sketch endpoints is the exact type of 'why is this different?' friction the 80-anchor describes, and the relation-echo inconsistency (enum type name vs relation ID, nested vs top-level) compounds it. A developer building a client against these endpoints would encounter enough irregularities that they cannot predict the exact shape without consulting implementation details, warranting a score in the low 70s rather than the 80-range.

strengths: Security model is explicitly documented at the class level (WordSketchApiServer Javadoc): loopback-only bind, intentionally unauthenticated, with a concrete warning about what must accompany any bind-address change.

All route registrations go through a single shared helper (registerHandler), making it structurally impossible for one route to silently acquire different method-enforcement or CORS behaviour than its siblings.
CORS header injection is centralised in setCorsHeader() and called by every response method, eliminating per-handler drift.

Input validation (size limits, parameter validation) is uniformly applied via shared utilities (requireParam, parseIntParam, readBodyWithSizeLimit) — no sibling endpoint skips validation while others apply it.
authorization_consistency.issue_character: The single issue is a missing startup guard: the CORS wildcard path is a latent trust-boundary widening that activates silently via a JVM property with only a log warning, rather than a fail-fast check.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase fully satisfies the core of the authorization_consistency rubric: the auth posture (intentionally unauthenticated, loopback-only) is uniform across all routes, documented, and structurally enforced through shared registration helpers. This places it comfortably above the 80 anchor ('solid but uneven'). The gap from 90+ is the cors.allow.origin=* path, which can silently widen the browser trust boundary across every endpoint at startup without any hard guardrail — a developer reviewing security cannot fully trust coverage once the server is deployed outside the default loopback configuration.

issues.summary: RelationType enum conflates dispatch-strategy values (SURFACE, DEP) with semantic-role labels (ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF), blurring the ownership boundary between the sketch routing and exploration subsystems
issues.suggestion: Split RelationType into two types: a MatchingStrategy enum (SURFACE, DEP) used by SketchHandlers and RelationConfig for dispatch, and a SemanticRole enum (ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF) used by exploration and comparison code for filtering. RelationConfig can then carry both — optional MatchingStrategy for sketch routing, optional SemanticRole for exploration — making each axis independently clear. Alternatively, add a javadoc comment to the enum explicitly documenting the dual-axis design and which values belong to which axis as an intermediate step.
evidence: SketchHandlers uses RelationType.SURFACE and RelationType.DEP exclusively as routing discriminators (e.g. 'if (relationType == RelationType.DEP)') with no semantic meaning.
CollocateProfileComparator and SemanticFieldExplorer use RelationType.ADJ_MODIFIER, ADJ_PREDICATE, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF exclusively as semantic category filters with no routing meaning.
The enum's javadoc ('Relation matching strategy: surface token sequence vs. dependency annotation') only documents the SURFACE/DEP axis, leaving ADJ_PREDICATE etc. unexplained in context.
A new engineer encountering the enum cannot determine which values are routing tags and which are semantic categories, because the two sets serve completely different call sites with no shared logic.

issues.summary: RelationConfig javadoc falsely claims deriveDeprel() 'has no callers outside this class' while SketchHandlers calls it in three places, making the documented ownership contract untrustworthy
issues.suggestion: Update the RelationConfig javadoc to accurately state that deriveDeprel() is part of the public API of RelationConfig and is used by SketchHandlers. If the original design intent was to keep it internal, move the deprel-derivation logic to RelationPatternBuilder (which already owns all other RelationConfig-derived computations) and update callers accordingly.
evidence: RelationConfig.java lines 14-16: 'The sole remaining method (deriveDeprel()) is retained here because it is a simple derivation from pattern() and id() that is only called during construction and has no callers outside this class.'
SketchHandlers.java line 94: rel.deriveDeprel() called for serializing deprel into response JSON.
SketchHandlers.java line 148: rel.deriveDeprel() used as a filter predicate in executeRelationQueries.
SketchHandlers.java line 274: rel.deriveDeprel() called again during response building.

issues.summary: RelationConfig javadoc cross-references GrammarConfig#getRelation but the actual method is named GrammarConfig#relation(String), making the doc-to-runtime contract unverifiable
issues.suggestion: Change the {@link} reference in RelationConfig.java line 43 from 'GrammarConfig#getRelation' to 'GrammarConfig#relation(String)' to match the actual API.
evidence: RelationConfig.java line 43: '{@link pl.marcinmilkowski.word_sketch.config.GrammarConfig#getRelation}' in the deriveDeprel() nullability design note.
GrammarConfig.java exposes 'public Optional<RelationConfig> relation(String id)' — no method named getRelation exists.

issues.summary: @NonNull annotation on parseSeedSet contradicts live null check in the method body
issues.suggestion: Choose one convention and apply it consistently. If the intent is that callers must never pass null (enforced by @NonNull), remove the null branch and keep only the isEmpty() check. If null is a legitimate input, remove the @NonNull annotation. The rest of the codebase uses @NonNull correctly (e.g. ExploreResponseAssembler parameters), so removing the annotation is the lower-friction fix here.
evidence: Line 233: `private static Set<String> parseSeedSet(@NonNull String seedsParam)` — @NonNull promises callers the parameter is non-null.
Line 234: `if (seedsParam == null || seedsParam.isEmpty()) return Set.of();` — null branch is dead code given the annotation, but signals defensive coding intent.

The two conventions — annotation-contract and defensive null check — are contradictory: one says callers must not pass null, the other says null is handled. A developer reading this cannot determine the intended contract.

issues.summary: package-info.java convention applied to model package only; six other packages lack it
issues.suggestion: Either add package-info.java to the remaining packages (particularly api/ and exploration/ which have the most non-obvious structural decisions) or note in model/package-info.java that it is the single authoritative architecture document. The exploration/ package's SemanticFieldExplorer-as-facade pattern especially benefits from documentation.
evidence: model/package-info.java exists and documents package boundaries, type-strategy rationale, and naming conventions.

No package-info.java exists in api/, config/, exploration/, query/, utils/, or indexer/ — packages with equally non-obvious boundary decisions (e.g. why api/ mixes handlers with assemblers, why exploration/ separates MultiSeedExplorer from SemanticFieldExplorer).
